Beyond factor exposure, AVDV also checks the practical boxes. It’s got $9.5B in AUM, which leagues ahead of most peers. This means tighter spreads, better liquidity, and lower risk of closure. The fee is a clean 0.36%, which undercuts some of the higher-cost options like First Trust Developed Markets ex-US Small Cap AlphaDEX® Fund ETF (FDTS) (0.80%) without sacrificing on design. While it’s not the oldest fund in the lineup, its 2019 launch hasn’t stopped it from scaling fast and gaining traction with advisors and quants alike. Compared to under-the-radar picks with low AUM or weak backing, AVDV offers both academic rigor and staying power.

Focusing more on diversification. AVDV spreads its bets across a wide range of cyclical sectors like Industrials, Materials, Financials, Consumer Cyclical without overloading any single one. The exposure is broad enough to capture diversified global small-cap value, yet focused enough to stay true to its mandate. You get real factor purity, but without putting all your risk in one bucket.

Regarding the equity holdings itself, AVDV is deep. Its top 10 holdings make up just over 8% of the fund. No single name dominates, and the largest weight is barely above 1%. With over 1,400 positions, you're getting true small-cap breadth across developed markets. This isn't a concentrated bet. It's a diversified tilt toward value that spreads risk across the globe.

Conclusion

For investors looking to complement core equity exposure with something that offers real diversification and disciplined factor exposure, Developed Markets Small Cap Value is a smart tilt and AVDV is one of the cleanest ways to express it. You're getting exposure to proven return drivers like value, size, and profitability, wrapped in a fund that's liquid, diversified, and cost-effective. It’s not always the fastest horse in the race, but over the right cycles, DM SCV has delivered, especially when US dominance takes a breather. If you believe in global equity premiums and want something that moves to its own rhythm, AVDV deserves a seat at the table.

I personally hold AVDV in my own globally diversified portfolio and am happy to be covering it again. Since my last article, AVDV has delivered 22.31% while the market benchmark delivered 1.68%.

I'm quite happy with the performance thus far and gladly dollar-cost-averaging into this wonderful fund.

Attribution

I'm very much a data-focused investor and want to credit the following sources that helped me generate the charts, graphs and content for this article:

  • FactorLytics - factorlytics.io
  • Seeking Alpha
  • Portfolio Visualizer

data.title: SEALSQ: The Most Practical Quantum Play
data.description:
SEALSQ (NASDAQ:LAES) is a more unusual approach to quantum computing as an investment: cybersecurity. I covered it back in February and decided to rate it a Hold.

Shares have been climbing over the past month too, up about 73%. I've found that much of this seems to follow the general sentiment around quantum. SEALSQ, however, remains a long-term contender for its practical cybersecurity product. The only tricky part is the cash flow and the valuation, and that is why I maintained my Hold rating.

Summary of Previous Thesis

Last time, I was talking about SEALSQ as it was coming out of 2024, a transitional year for the company. This was marked by much lower revenue and gross profits for the reported period.

A fabless maker of microchips for classical computers, the transition was toward a "post-quantum" product. Specifically, it's a type of semiconductor that can be built into a classical computer, to protect it from the cryptographic power that quantum computing has over classical.

Therefore, my thesis was that SEALSQ should be treated as relevant because, while its product wasn't quantum, it had a role to play in protecting current technology on a cybersecurity basis. With that said, the lack of free cash flow and potential for more share dilution as the stock rallied (temporarily) left me rating it a Hold.

Full-Year 2024 Results

As the semi-annual report I cited earlier foreshadowed, full-year 2024 results show much higher operating losses, primarily due to the revenue decline and the transitional nature of that year. It should be noted that revenue was naturally lower, given the cyclical nature of the semiconductor industry.

Nevertheless, the balance sheet showed improvement from the ATM equity offerings.

They ended the year with $84.6M in cash, retiring about $6M in debt down to a balance of $3.1M.

The YoY cash burn increased, primarily as operating cash flows grew from a burn of $3M to $11.2M.

Overall, a transition year doesn't boast great results for almost any company, but they did intelligently take advantage of the quantum stock rally last fall, giving them a very strong cash position.

Current Outlook

Being a Swiss company, they do not release Form 10Q every quarter, but they have provided more updates, with guidance, about halfway through the year.

As of May, management has indicated a booking growth of 80% compared to 2024, indicating that revenue should be recovering this year, and the three-year pipeline stands at a hopeful $93M.

The strategy for the year ahead and after has four key points:

  • QUASAR Program (quantum products)
  • OSPT Centers
  • Investment in quantum companies
  • Satellite connectivity

The use of quantum-resistant chips I already mentioned. The OSPT centers will be facilities located in different countries, in order to design and create chips that fit customer specifications. Investment in quantum speaks for itself; they think there is an opportunity for the same reason they are redesigning the chips. Satellite connectivity is pointed at their IoT market (Internet of Things) products, aiming to provide secure connections to IoT devices around the world.

During the Q4 earnings call, CEO Carlos Moreira laid out SEALSQ's value proposition with this strategy:

It's important to note that any customer that is running major infrastructure, whether that comes from hospitality or whatever it comes from hotels and client security, whatever is required for airport medical facility, government facility, all of them, they are now looking into upgrade their facility to TPM levels to be able to defend a quantum attack.

It will be too late if these organizations wait until the quantum computers are available to start defending their infrastructure. So the Quantum resilient strategy will soon be forced and not only a nice to have, but a must-have in order to comply with many of the current cybersecurity criteria.

This indicates that, while many quantum companies are years out from meaningful revenues, this doesn't have to be the case for SEALSQ. I'll also reference CFO John O'Hara's remarks about the pipeline:

So this projection as I say, revenue can ramp up very fast here as Post-Quantum is going to be compulsory. I mean, in September this year, there's going to be regulatory moves by forcing companies to be quantum resilient compliance. Otherwise, their insurance premium will be increased, and they might be, in some cases, not even able to sell their object anymore.

I'm also going to quote an observation I made in my last thesis, before this earnings call broadcast:

...Berkshire tends to avoid writing cybersecurity insurance because the upside of the potential claims is unpredictably high. A single cybersecurity breach has the potential to compromise an entire business operation, posing a large liability.

I think there's a lot more potential in the underlying product here. One doesn't have to bet on a specific type of quantum computer, as they do with other quantum computing stocks.

As the OSPT centers allow them to expand their geographic footprint, there's reason to think that they reach revenues above 2023's levels. I just don't think it will necessarily be quick.

Valuation

Currently, LAES's price of around $4 per share gives it a market cap near $500M, but is this a company earning at least $50M? Not currently.

With gross margins in 2023 around 50%, the volume would probably need to bring them to $50M in revenue to breakeven. LAES is investing for growth, but it doesn't seem like 2025 is the year when sales explode.

With $20M of their cash set aside for quantum investment, they only went into 2025 with about $60M free. They've begun the process to acquire IC' ALPS, so it's likely more capital will need to be raised to invest in the growth they want. They already may be in the pattern of selling more shares, as they did in a direct offering that raised $20M in May.

All of this to say, with the cash flows still negative, more spending for growth ahead, and the temptation to take advantage of the higher stock price, there's good reason to think more dilution could occur and nullify the upside on the market cap.

For that reason, I think the shares are only fairly valued.

Conclusion

LAES continues to be a practical quantum play, in the long term. This is because they build on an existing semiconductor product for classical computers and because it doesn't need to anticipate too much. By focusing on security, it simply needs to protect against the cryptographic side of quantum.

With the current state of the cash flow, however, it's difficult to see the upside not being threatened by further share dilution, and for that reason, I have declined to upgrade and maintain my Hold rating.

data.title: Can The Promise Of Profitability And Pipelines Sustain A Recovery For Esperion Therapeutics?
data.description:
Esperion Therapeutics, Inc (NASDAQ:ESPR) has been running a gauntlet of biotech and healthcare conferences over the past several weeks. The stock has actually responded positively. Still, while it is nice to see a timely 64% rebound off the all-time low setback in October 2023, I am left wondering why the stock got THIS low in the first place. After ESPR last failed to hold the important $1.50 support level that I have written about, I pretty much assumed this boom or bust speculative play on cardiovascular therapeutics started a plunge toward the bust side of the ledger. Now, with a June 3rd $16 price target in place from Wainwright following its biotech investor conference, I am reviving the abiding hope that motivated me to lean against the abiding skepticism in the stock so evident from the poor price action over the last 2+ years.

There are three key points I have learned/observed over the last three conferences that seem to offer new news that I did not find in the last earnings call (readers, please correct me as needed):

  • Around mid-March, prescription growth picked up and started exceeding expectations, thus suggesting a resumption in business momentum.
  • Esperion projected profitability in Q1 2026
  • Esperion guided to U.S. product revenue of $165 to $175M

Moreover, a pipeline of future therapies for liver and kidney maladies seems to be materializing enough to matter for the stock. The company held an R&D day on April 24 to discuss the pipeline for liver therapeutics, but the subsequent rally lasted less than two weeks before the stock sank to its test of all-time lows. Success in the pipeline should make Esperion more attractive by an order of magnitude.

My following commentary combines the information learned across the three investor conferences:

  • May 20, 2025: H.C. Wainwright’s 3rd Annual BioConnect Investor Conference
  • June 4, 2025: Jefferies Global Healthcare Conference
  • June 9, 2025: Goldman Sachs Global Healthcare Conference

Prescription growth

Esperion offers two drugs, Nexletol and Nexlizet, both based on bempedoic acid. Nexletol has the following indications and usage (from the U.S. prescription guidance sheet):

NEXLETOL, an adenosine triphosphate-citrate lyase (ACL) inhibitor, is indicated:

  • To reduce the risk of myocardial infarction and coronary revascularization in adults who are unable to take recommended statin therapy (including those not taking a statin) with:
    • established cardiovascular disease (CVD), or
    • a high risk for a CVD event but without established CVD. (1)
  • As an adjunct to diet, in combination with other low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) lowering therapies, or alone when concomitant LDL-C lowering therapy is not possible, to reduce LDL-C in adults with primary hyperlipidemia, including heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HeFH).

Nexlizet has the following indications and usage (from the U.S. prescription guidance sheet):

NEXLIZET, a combination of bempedoic acid, an adenosine triphosphate citrate lyase (ACL) inhibitor, and ezetimibe, a dietary cholesterol absorption inhibitor, is indicated:

  • As an adjunct to diet, alone or in combination with other low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) lowering therapies, to reduce LDL-C in adults with primary hyperlipidemia, including heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HeFH). The bempedoic acid component of NEXLIZET is indicated:
  • To reduce the risk of myocardial infarction and coronary revascularization in adults who are unable to take recommended statin therapy (including those not taking a statin) with:
    • established cardiovascular disease (CVD), or
    • a high risk for a CVD event but without established CVD.

Bempedoic acid performs the same function in both drugs.

A label expansion last year opened up a 70 million patient opportunity (190M lives are covered by the new label) in the U.S. that the company talks about every chance it gets. It is a tantalizing prospect given Esperion’s scripts reached 220,000 cumulative in Q1 2025. The company also has a slowly expanding international footprint that includes 472,500 cumulative European patients in February 2025.

The resulting revenue growth was impressive until a speed bump in Q1 related to confusion with Medicare coverage. Medicare issues now seem to be in the rearview mirror. One hundred dollar co-pays for Medicare patients are down to $0 to $40, the company has “never seen a $0 co-pay before.”

Esperion seems to be back on track with what the company describes as a targeted focus on statin intolerant patients starting in mid-March. CEO Sheldon King described a sales pitch that creates a sense of urgency: “We know 30% of the people in your office are statin intolerant. How many of them need to get to LDL-C goal in order to avoid risk of a cardiovascular event? Why should we wait until they’ve had the heart attack to try to prevent the next one?”

Even before the “intolerance blitz”, Halladay disclosed that new subscribers (those who have not written a prescription in the last 12 months) per week doubled and in some cases tripled.

With revenue growth picking up and exceeding expectations, CFO Ben Halladay described Q2 as “shaping up to be a fantastic quarter.”

Profitability

During the earnings conference call, management spoke generally about profitability as a part of its “three pillars for growth; revenue growth, portfolio expansion, and pipeline advancement.” During the Wainwright conference, Halladay described the company’s profitability as “almost there”. As if anticipating the concerns behind the question about profitability, he added, “by the time we have to start funding anything in the pipeline, profitability will be so far behind us that it won’t even be an issue. It will be entirely self-funded.” He further reassured that the company is “in great shape when it comes to trajectory for profitability and cash flow.”

During the Jeffries conference, CEO Sheldon King also spoke on profitability. He said he is “comfortable with consensus growth estimates out on the street, growth will continue to accelerate. Based on the rebound from the first quarter, we will be profitable by the first quarter of 2026.” King also clarified that this profitability does not include the $130M milestone payment ESPR anticipates in Q4 from Otsuka Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd (during the Goldman conference King said this milestone payment is triggered on pricing, and he feels confident the trigger will be met with Japanese regulators).

I sat up straight when I heard these claims about profitability, but I am having a hard time seeing how the numbers work out (and I would love some feedback on what I might be missing here!). Here are how the numbers stack up with FY 2025 guidance and the current cash position:

  • OpEx: $215 – $235M
  • SG&A: $160 – $170M
  • R&D: $55 – $65M
  • Revenue from bempedoic acid: $165 – $170M (revealed/confirmed in the Jeffries conference)
  • Current cash: $114M

Note how SG&A alone eats up all the revenue from bempedoic acid drugs; at the high-end, total expected expenditures for 2025 come to $430M. Total revenue in Q1 was $65.0M, with net (U.S.) product sales at $34.9M. If Esperion is going to achieve Q1 profitability next year, then I must be missing some large recurring revenue source that suddenly kicks into high gear in Q1.

Current cash, plus product revenue, plus the promised $130M from Otsuka comes to $414M at the high end, short of the low-end of expected expenditures. Esperion will be skating on a razor-thin margin going into Q1 profitability. I looked and looked through the last 10Q in search of answers and found none (for example, I did not see a specific call-out for some expected major boost in recurring revenue or recurring collaboration payments). Anyone who knows what these numbers are missing, please post along with a reference (thanks in advance!). Without this mysterious revenue source, I find profitability in Q1 2026 hard to believe and am even worried the company is cutting things too closely to avoid a fresh round of financing in 2025.

The following disclosure in the 10Q provides reassurances that the company should NOT need to do another financing round. It also suggests that milestone payments are a part of staying liquid this year:

“We expect to continue to incur expenses and operating losses for the near term future in connection with our continued commercialization activities associated with NEXLETOL and NEXLIZET in the U.S. Pursuant to the license and collaboration agreements with DSE, Otsuka, and DS, we are eligible for substantial additional sales and regulatory milestone payments and royalties. We estimate that current cash resources, proceeds to be received in the future for product sales and proceeds under the collaboration agreements with DSE, DS and Otsuka are sufficient to fund operations for the foreseeable future.”

I cannot connect the dots from here to profitability. Is the stock performing so poorly because others share my wariness?

Pipeline

During earnings, Esperion referred to the R&D Day discussion about new liver therapies. In the Q1 earnings presentation, the company provided the following view into its pipeline:

Of course, King had promising prognostications about the pipeline.

The triple combination will be “unveiled” later this year. It features a 70-80% reduction in LDL-C in one pill. King claims the pill will offer “the single most efficacious LDL-C pill on the market.” The pill offers a great adherence benefit. It simplifies the work for payers. Moreover, Esperion only needs to conduct a bioequivalence study to get to market, thus associated development expenses are minimal. This triple combo pill could extend patent protection for partner Daichii for 5–10 years.

The liver therapy for Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) impacts liver bioducts, inflammation, healing from the injury, and cystic fibrosis (CF). Esperion has found an intersection between metabolism and cell fibrosis. The company believes it has a drug that could cure this orphan disease and at least postpone transplantation. The therapy may even have an oncology effect.

Esperion has “an entire kidney program” that it will talk about next year.

Competition

Competition may be an area where a wary market is significantly discounting Esperion’s potential.

King noted that it has 2 of 9 settlements in place over the NDA on generic Nexletol (at no cost). A good argument apparently exists to get to 2040 patent protection (the current target is 2031). King claims the manufacturing patents are strong, especially given it is difficult and dangerous to make the drug. Assuming filers are acknowledging this reality, he expects further settlements in short order.

Starting in 2028, Esperion might see competition with drugs like New Amsterdam, oral PCSK9, and others. King insisted that “all new medications are welcome.” However, he referred to 4 prior drugs that “never made it.” He also referred to the literature on New Amsterdam to cast some doubts but acknowledged that questions will not be answered until late 2026 or early 2027. King also cautioned that oral PCSK9 requires fasting for 8 hours.

Conclusion: Now the Wait for Profitability

The recent revival from the depth has revived my interest and hope in ESPR. I even added more shares to my remaining core position after I sold enough shares in the December breakout to pay for my cost basis at the time. However, the breakdown from the $1.50 presumed support level means ESPR has a LONG and arduous road ahead for recovery. Wainwright’s street high price target of $16 helped push ESPR over the $1.00 mark, but the stock is already stalling out at current levels. Thus, I have to assume the abiding skepticism about ESPR is as strong as ever. Whereas I thought the expanded label and then a successful expansion of the sales force would provide sustainable upside catalysts, now I have to look forward to proof positive of profitability as the next potential sustainable catalyst.

Be careful out there!

data.title: 2 Under-The-Radar Net Lease REITs That Are Poised To Profit
data.description:
A few days ago, I wrote an article titled "Nothing But Net (Lease REITs)" in which I highlighted a few of my favorite freestanding REITs based on their "wide moat" advantages.

As you can see, Agree Realty (ADC), Essential Properties (EPRT), Four Corners (FCPT), VICI Properties (VICI) and Realty Income (O) all stand out for their lower WACC (weighted average cost of capital) and ability to generate peer-beating investment spreads.

Although net lease REITs have underperformed other property sectors over the last few years, historically, the "steady Eddie" property category has generated a respectable 10% annualized (based on total returns) over the last 15 years.

While many dividend investors own these net lease REITs for their high dividend yields and steady dividend growth, some investors scout the REIT universe for bargains, after all, everyone likes to have their cake and eat it too.

Over the years, I've made some good money off of deeply valued net lease REITs.

For example, I recommended Four Corners Property Trust (FCPT) during COVID, in March 2020, as I explained,

"…we believe that, because of FCPT's strong financial condition, it will likely pursue concessions such as rent reductions in exchange for lease extensions. Because of its "circle of competence" as a restaurant landlord, the REIT is perfectly positioned to strategically amend leases in its favor. Unlike EPR (EPR)."

That Strong Buy call was spot on, as FCPT later soared from $13.78 to around $30.00 by the end of 2020, returning over 113%.

Not bad for a net lease REIT…

Forgive me for cherry-picking, so let me provide you with another example of a net lease that I recommend that delivered very solid returns.

In late December 2018, I initiated coverage of Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) with a STRONG Buy rating, and I said that the company warrants a Strong Buy because it could return 25% in 12 months.

As you can see below, that Strong Buy pick was also Spot on" as FCPT generated 87% total returns in 2019.

So, you see, it is possible to "have your cake and eat it too" when it comes to net lease REIT investing, but you have to know where to find the buried gold…and in this article, I'm going to provide you with my road map.

FrontView REIT (FVR)

FrontView REIT was formed in 2016 as a private REIT, and by 2023, the company had acquired around $800 million in gross assets. In October 2024, the company listed on the NYSE at $19 per share, and the recent closing price was $11.33 per share.

As you can see, FrontView is the worst-performing net lease REIT YTD, down around 36%, which leads me to the next question: "what's wrong with this REIT?"

Upon closer inspection, FrontView now owns 307 stand-alone properties (over 2.4 mm sq.ft.) leased to 150 different brands in 35 states. The portfolio consists of 33% IG (investment grade) customers with an average remaining lease term of 7.2 years and average occupancy of 98%.

FrontView's investment strategy is part of its name; the company seeks to own properties located along high-traffic (frontage) corridors in which the building facades are highly visible, allowing the tenants to advertise their brands on the buildings.

Importantly, FrontView's highest tenant concentration is 2.9%, with just 21.8% concentration within the company's top 10 list. This makes FrontView one of the most diversified net lease REITs in terms of tenant concentration.

Compare that "top 10" tenant concentration with these peers:

So, as you can see, FrontView has better diversification than all of the net lease REIT peers, with just 21.8% concentration with the "top 10". Also, I find it interesting that FrontView provides the details for its "top 40" customers, which is an added level of transparency you don't get with the other net lease REIT peers.

In terms of direct peers, I would classify FrontView of something of a hybrid between Realty Income and Agree Realty - that have 34% and 68% investment grade customers respectively - and NNN REIT (NNN) and Essential Properties (EPRT) - that have private equity backed customers that operate car washes, casual dining restaurants, and QSRs.

I see nothing wrong with FrontView's business model, and in fact, I like these smaller +/- $1 mm ticket sites in which the land is typically worth as much as the building in many cases.

These are smaller bets, so in addition to the enhanced customer diversification attributes, the smaller deals help to mitigate risk in the event of a tenant default.

Take it from me, as an experienced net lease developer for over two decades, I would much rather own a vacant freestanding building on my major corridor than a big box in the middle of nowhere when the tenant blows out.

And FrontView has held up fairly well with 98% occupancy as of Q1-25.

However, on the latest earnings call, FrontView management said it had taken back or planned to take back a Freddy's, two TGIF Fridays, four Hooters, three On The Border, and a Joanne Fabrics, collectively around 4% of ABR.

The company said that two of its four Hooters could be candidates for leases, post-bankruptcy, with Hooters. In addition, management said it "expects that a substantial majority of these properties should be back online in late 2025 at meaningful recovery rates."

Most of the pain comes from the casual dining space, which represents around 15% of ABR (down from 19.3% at the end of Q3-24).

Another category that FrontView and other net lease REITs are cognizant of is the drug store category. FrontView has been reducing exposure there as well. As of Q1-25, the company owned three Walgreens and four CVS properties, representing around 3.3% of ABR.

At the end of Q4, FrontView acquired 29 properties at an average price of $3.6 million (12 tenants) and 27% investment grade rated.

At the end of the quarter, the company had $68.5 million drawn on its $250 million revolving line of credit, and the company has no near-term debt maturities. Also, as shown below, OP units represent ~25% of the capital stack.

FrontView initiated 2025 guidance of $20.00 to $26.00 per share, with acquisitions of between $175 million to $200 million and dispositions of $5 million to $20 million.

The Q4024 AFFO per share was 0.33, and the quarterly dividend was $.215, which translates into a healthy payout ratio of 65%.

Now, clearly, the casual dining sector has resulted in the above-referenced selloff; however, it appears that FrontView should be able to easily mitigate pressures because of its diversified asset base.

And the valuation is tasty…

Shares are now trading at $11.33 per share, with a dividend yield of 7.6%.

Now take a look at FrontView's P/FFO multiple compared with its peers:

FrontView is a new player in REIT-dom, and there hasn't been much coverage here on Seeking Alpha. Just two recommendations: 1 Buy and 1 Sell.

However, we now have a third recommendation (on FVR), and this one is a Strong Buy. If the company can manage its watch list customers, which it should be able to do give the experienced management team, shares could fetch $17.00, which translates into annualized returns of 40% to 50%.

Modiv Industrial (MDV)

Modiv Industrial is a net lease REIT that invests in industrial manufacturing facilities to companies that make products that support and strengthen our nation".

Although not as diversified as FrontView, Modiv has carved out an interesting niche by investing in private-equity backed customers that produce "Great American" essentials in the infrastructure, auto parts, aerospace/defense, technology hardware, and light metal fabrication sectors.

Modiv owns 43 properties (4.5 mm sq.ft) leased to 29 tenants in 15 states. 39 of the properties are considered industrial properties (80% of ABR) along with four non-core properties (20% of ABR).

Approximately 30% of the tenants or their parent companies have an investment-grade credit rating from a recognized credit agency of BBB or better.

It's worth noting that Modiv's largest customer, Lindsay, generates 14% of ABR, and its second-largest customer, KIA of Carson, generates 11% of ABR. As Austin Rogers explains,

"Lindsay Precast, produces concrete and steel molds for highway guardrails, overpasses, commercial construction projects, and utility equipment pads. It has a long history of operating at the locations MDV now owns, and it could even benefit from increased infrastructure spending in the US."

I looked in the latest annual report to determine what Modiv owns (Kia of Carson) and it appears to be a dealership that was built in 2016 and acquired by Modiv in January 2022 for around $70 mm.

So, Modiv fished in a smaller pond than other peers, as I would consider Gladstone Commercial (GOOD) and Broadstone Net Lease (BNL) closer peers.

Modiv's portfolio is valued at around $602 mm and generates around $39 mm in annual rent. The weighted average lease term is 14.2 years, with 2.5% average annual rent increases.

Concerning the balance sheet and liquidity, at the end of Q1-25, Modiv had total cash and cash equivalents of $6.2 million and $280 million of debt outstanding ($31 million of mortgages on 2 properties and $250 million of outstanding borrowings on the $280 million credit facility).

Importantly, Modiv has higher leverage than most peers:

In Q1-25, Modiv's AFFO was $3.9 million, up 18% compared with $3.3 million in Q1-24. The increase in AFFO was related to a $195,000 increase in cash rental income, a $200,000 decrease in cash interest expense, and $140,000 decrease in property expenses.

On a per-share basis, AFFO was just $.03 per share because of an increase of 483,000 shares from ATM issuances, Class X OP units issued to employees in Q1, and shares issued from an UPREIT transaction.

Modiv has around $45 million on 7.375% Series A preferred shares (9% of capitalization) as well as around 2.5 mm shares in OP units. I consider OP units opportunistic for REITs and property owners; however, it's important that such OP transactions are accretive.

I do like the fact that insiders hold around 8.7% of shares outstanding and insiders such as CEO, Aaron Halfacre, and Director, Gingras Raymond, are busy buying.

In addition to tenant concentration and higher leverage, I also want to point out the lumpy earnings history. As shown below, the company's AFFO has been up and down, which puts more pressure on the dividend payments.

Importantly, Modiv stands for MOnthly DIVidends, which makes the company a retail-oriented investment alternative like Realty Income (O), Agree Realty (ADC), STAG Industrial (STAG), and Healthpeak (DOC), to name a few.

Of course, I would rather have quality than quantity, and I think you would too.

Using analyst consensus data (above chart), Modiv's 2025 payout ratio is around 83%, which seems reasonable given its industrial tenant composition. In addition, the consensus growth estimate for 2026 is 8%, which suggests Modiv can continue boosting its dividend.

In terms of valuation, shares are trading at $14.12 with a dividend yield of 8.1%, one of the highest in the net lease sector. And the P/FFO multiple is the second lowest (in the peer group) as shown below.

Although there's enhanced risk (leverage, tenant concentration, OP dilution, compensation), I believe Modiv warrants a speculative Strong Buy. Shares could fetch $20.00, which translates into a 12-month return of 40%.

In Closing

Keep in mind, small-cap stocks are riskier stocks, and as a responsible investor, you should always maintain reasonable diversification.

I hope you enjoyed reading this article, and I look forward to your comments below. As always, thank you for the opportunity to be of service.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street on Friday plummeted, giving up all its gains for the week and ending in the red.

The decline was almost single-handedly due to a sharp escalation between Israel and Iran, with both countries trading missile strikes and stoking concerns of an all-out war in the Middle East. Oil prices (CL1:COM)(CO1:COM) spiked over supply worries, while traders flocked to traditional safe-haven asset gold (XAUUSD:CUR).

Aside from geopolitics, this week's spotlight was on some key inflation data and further trade developments with China.

U.S. President Donald Trump on Wednesday declared that a deal with China was "done, subject to final approval" from him and the Asian nation's leader, Xi Jinping. The terms include China providing rare earths to the U.S., a factor that has been a wrangling point in negotiations before.

The May consumer price index and producer price index reports were published this week. Both sets of readings came in softer than anticipated, and, coupled with signs of cooling in the labor market, led traders to raise their Federal Reserve rate cut expectations.

Boeing (BA) was also in the news. One of the U.S. planemaker's Dreamliner jets was involved in a crash in India on Thursday that left hundreds dead.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) slipped -0.4%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) shed -0.6%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) fell -1.3%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.
